findings.title: stripEmbeddedBismillah never matches the live API text — the headline double-Bismillah fix is a no-op
findings.description: Commit d8bdc72's main reader fix ('strip the Bismillah alquran.cloud embeds in ayah 1... cache bumped to v3') does nothing. The EMBEDDED_BISMILLAH constant encodes fatha-before-shadda (…0x644 0x64E 0x651 0x647…) but the live quran-uthmani edition returns shadda-before-fatha (…0x644 0x651 0x64E 0x647…) in both اللَّه and الرَّحْمَٰن/الرَّحِيم. Verified by fetching surah 2 ayah 1 from api.alquran.cloud right now: t.startsWith(EMBEDDED_BISMILLAH) === False. So every non-9 surah in Uthmani mode STILL renders the Bismillah twice (fixed header + embedded copy in ayah 1), the exact round-1 complaint, and the v3 cache bump invalidated all users' cached surahs for nothing — the un-stripped text is now re-cached under v3, so a later fix needs another bump. Note the comment 'Exact codepoints verified against the live API' is false.
findings.suggestedFix: Compare in a canonical form: const norm = (s: string) => s.normalize('NFC'); if (norm(t).startsWith(norm(EMBEDDED_BISMILLAH))) ... (NFC reorders the combining marks consistently since fatha ccc=30 < shadda ccc=33). If Hermes' normalize support is in doubt, strip/ignore combining marks (U+064B–U+0652, U+0670) when prefix-matching and slice the original string by counted base+mark chars. Bump CACHE_VERSION to 4 so already-recached v3 entries are refetched, and add a unit test pinning the live API codepoints.
findings.correction: Claim is accurate in substance. Two refinements: (a) likely root cause — the round-1 audit doc (docs/audit-2026-06-10.json) itself recorded the API text in NFC order (َّ), so the constant was probably copied from normalized audit text rather than actual wire bytes, explaining the false 'verified against the live API' comment; (b) simplest fix is to NFC-normalize the incoming text (or compare on normalize('NFC')) before startsWith — the existing constant is exactly the NFC form of the live prefix — plus a CACHE_VERSION bump to v4. Scope nit: 'every non-9 surah' should read surahs 2-114 except 9 (surah 1 is intentionally excluded by the guard since 1:1 IS the Bismillah).

findings.title: AyahSheet playAyah has no generation/cancel guard — phantom audio after closing the sheet or switching verses while loading
findings.description: Audio.Sound.createAsync({ shouldPlay: true }) takes seconds on a slow network. If the user closes the sheet (closeSheet → stopAyah sees soundRef.current === null → no-op → onClose) or taps a different verse while the request is in flight, the cleanup effect on [verse?.verseKey] unloads the (still-null) ref, then createAsync resolves and the sound starts playing with no owner: the sheet is gone, nothing stops it until the clip ends — and on a rapid verse switch two sounds overlap. This is exactly the bug class d8bdc72 fixed in useAudioPlayer with genRef ('no phantom playback after Stop') but AyahSheet, touched in the same commit, got no equivalent guard.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a generation counter (genRef) bumped in playAyah, stopAyah, and the verse-change/unmount cleanup; after createAsync resolves, if the gen changed, sound.unloadAsync() and return instead of assigning soundRef.current. Alternatively capture the verseKey at call time and bail if it no longer matches the open verse.
findings.correction: Two refinements: (1) Blast radius is self-limiting — the phantom sound is a single ayah clip (seconds long) and is also unloaded if the user reopens the sheet on any verse (the verseKey cleanup effect then has a non-null ref). The overlapping-two-sounds scenario requires a specific interleaving (close sheet + select another verse + tap play while the first request is still in flight), so 'high' is at the generous end; medium-high is more precise, though consistent with how d8bdc72 classified the same bug class. (2) The claim understates one variant: if the app is backgrounded while createAsync is in flight, the AppState listener is already removed (verse is null after onClose) and the global audio mode is staysActiveInBackground:true, so the phantom clip plays under the lock screen with no way to stop it.

findings.title: Qibla AppState retry re-opens the OS location permission dialog on every foreground while denied (Android prompt loop)
findings.description: Commit 3611603 added an AppState handler that calls retry() whenever the app foregrounds with permissionDenied/usingFallback. retry() re-runs useLocation's fetchLocation, which calls requestLocationPermissionOnce() — i.e. Location.requestForegroundPermissionsAsync() — unconditionally (useLocation.ts line 72), with no getForegroundPermissionsAsync pre-check. On Android, while the permission is denied (but not yet 'never ask again'), every return to the app with the Qibla screen open pops the OS permission dialog uninvited; worse, the dialog itself backgrounds the app, so tapping Deny fires 'active' and immediately re-prompts — a deny loop until Android auto-converts to permanent denial. This directly defeats the deliberate design in useCompass.ts (start(false) on resume, commented 'resume re-checks must not re-open the OS dialog') that landed in the same commit.
findings.suggestedFix: On resume, check Location.getForegroundPermissionsAsync() first and only call retry() if status is now 'granted' (mirroring useCompass.start(false)); keep the prompt-capable path solely behind the explicit warning-card tap. Or add a checkOnly refresh mode to useLocation that skips requestLocationPermissionOnce when status !== 'granted'.
findings.correction: Severity 'high' is at the upper bound; practical impact differs by platform/OS version. On Android 11+ (API 30+, the large majority of devices), two denials auto-convert to permanent denial — the first-launch prompt plus the Qibla-mount request typically consume both asks, so the foreground handler usually shows at most one uninvited dialog (the indefinite deny loop only manifests on Android <=10). The bigger modern-Android harm is that the uninvited re-prompt burns the user's last remaining ask, accelerating 'never ask again' and forcing Settings-only recovery. iOS is unaffected (the OS never re-shows the dialog after denial; the retry is a silent re-fetch). Secondary effect: in the usingFallback-but-granted state, the handler triggers an unthrottled GPS fetch on every foreground (battery waste, no dialog). Fix direction: gate the resume retry behind Location.getForegroundPermissionsAsync() (prompt only if status==='granted' changed, mirroring useCompass's start(false) pattern) and/or use the throttled refreshIfStale instead of retry.

findings.title: v3 cache bump leaves all quran_surah_v2_ entries orphaned forever — purgeQuranCacheOnce can never remove them
findings.description: CACHE_VERSION went 2→3 with no migration: nothing ever deletes the old quran_surah_v2_* keys (CACHE_VERSION_KEY is declared on line 9 and never used — the intended version-migration was never implemented). purgeQuranCacheOnce filters only on the CURRENT prefix (k.startsWith(CACHE_PREFIX) === quran_surah_v3_) and is gated by the quran_cache_purged_v1 flag, which every existing 1.0.7 install has already set — so it will never run again and wouldn't match v2 keys anyway. Every surah a user opened on the previous release leaves a dead ~30–80KB JSON permanently in AsyncStorage, eating into the 6MB Android SQLite cap that this very file documents as causing SQLITE_FULL failures for streak/goals/prayer-mark writes.
findings.suggestedFix: Actually use CACHE_VERSION_KEY: on startup, if the stored version !== CACHE_VERSION, getAllKeys() and multiRemove every key matching /^quran_surah_v\d+_/ that doesn't carry the current prefix (same for tajweed/page prefixes), then write the new version. Or simply broaden the purge filter to /^quran_surah_v/ and version the purge flag (quran_cache_purged_v2) whenever CACHE_VERSION bumps.
findings.correction: Real, but impact is bounded and the size estimate is off. prefetchAllSurahs is a no-op in 1.0.7, so only surahs the user explicitly opened after the one-shot purge are orphaned — typically a handful (tens to a few hundred KB of dead weight), not every install carrying significant bloat. Worst case (a heavy reader who opened many surahs) can reach multi-MB since each entry holds 3 editions, meaningfully eating the documented 6MB cap. The '~30-80KB per surah' figure understates long surahs (Al-Baqarah with Arabic+English+Urdu is several hundred KB) and overstates short ones (a few KB). Severity medium is defensible for the worst case; typical-case impact is low. Fix is trivial: drop the purge-flag prefix filter to 'quran_surah_v' (version-agnostic, matching the existing 'quran_page_v' pattern on line 533) plus a one-shot v2 sweep, or actually implement the intended CACHE_VERSION_KEY migration.

findings.title: Al-Fatiha renders the Bismillah twice: fixed header plus ayah 1 (which IS the Bismillah)
findings.description: stripEmbeddedBismillah deliberately exempts surah 1 (quran.ts line 78: `if (idx !== 0 || surahNumber === 1 || surahNumber === 9) return t;`) because 1:1 legitimately IS the Bismillah — but the reader still renders its own fixed Bismillah header for every surah except 9. So once the strip is fixed (finding above), Al-Fatiha will show the Bismillah as the header AND again as the numbered ayah-1 card. Same in the surah reader's mushaf view (line 842) and in MushafPageReader page 1 (head.surahNumber !== 9 banner + verse 1:1 text). Convention (quran.com, physical mushafs) is no separate unnumbered Bismillah for Fatiha.
findings.suggestedFix: Render the fixed Bismillah header only when selectedSurah.number !== 9 && selectedSurah.number !== 1 (both ScrollView branches in QuranScreen, and head.surahNumber !== 1 in MushafPageReader's banner block).
findings.correction: The claim's temporal framing is slightly off: it says 'once the strip is fixed (finding above), Al-Fatiha will show the Bismillah as the header AND again as ayah 1' — but the strip is already implemented at HEAD (commit d8bdc72), so this is not a future consequence of a pending fix; the Fatiha duplication exists in the current build right now (and also existed before the strip, since the header always rendered for surah 1). The fix is to also exclude surah 1 from the fixed header in QuranScreen.tsx (lines 842, 873) and MushafPageReader.tsx (line 445), not to change quran.ts — the surah-1 exemption in stripEmbeddedBismillah is correct and must stay.

findings.title: fetchJsonWithRetry retries permanent HTTP failures and stacks to ~31.5s before the error banner
findings.description: The retry loop treats every failure identically: a 4xx (permanent — bad surah/page/edition or API change) is retried after a 1.5s sleep, doubling latency for an outcome that cannot change; and on a fully hung network the worst case before 'Could not load. Check your internet connection.' is 15s timeout + 1.5s backoff + 15s timeout = 31.5s of 'Loading surah…'. Round 1's complaint was the infinite hang; the fix bounds it but the bound is long enough that users will background the app first, and HTTP 4xx wastes a guaranteed-futile second round trip.
findings.suggestedFix: Skip the retry for 4xx responses (throw immediately when res.status >= 400 && res.status < 500), and consider a shorter first-attempt timeout (e.g. 8s) so the retried worst case stays under ~25s.
findings.correction: Two scope narrowings: (1) fetchSurah is cache-first (quran.ts:140-151), so the 31.5s worst case only affects surahs with no valid cached copy — though the same commit bumped CACHE_VERSION to 3, invalidating all prior caches, so first opens after the update are network-bound. (2) The two sub-claims are independent: the 31.5s ceiling is timeout-driven, not 4xx-driven; a server answering 4xx promptly costs only ~1.5s + one RTT extra, and retry-on-4xx does not lengthen the hung-network case. Additionally, the other fetchJsonWithRetry call sites (lines 264, 394, 432 — Mushaf page, IndoPak, tajweed) surface their own fallback/retry UIs, so not every failure path is a 31.5s 'Loading surah…' spinner; the headline scenario applies mainly to the uncached surah-open path.

findings.title: Tasbih increment uses a non-functional setCount — a tap racing the hydration merge clobbers the restored count (then persists the loss)
findings.description: Commit 764ae67 made hydration use functional merges (`setCount((c) => c + (saved.count ?? 0))`, line 84) precisely so pre-hydration taps survive. But `increment()` itself uses a stale-closure, non-functional update: `const next = count + 1; setCount(next)`. Hydration's setState comes from a promise (default priority — React 18 schedules its render as a separate task, it is not flushed synchronously), so a bead tapped after hydration resolves but before that render commits runs with the last committed `count` (e.g. 0) and replaces the whole queue result: 0 → (hydration) 0+saved → (tap, non-functional) 1. The user's saved in-progress count visibly snaps back to 1 and the 600ms debounce flush then persists the loss permanently. Note `setLifetime((l) => l + 1)` and `setTodayCount((c) => c + 1)` two lines below are correctly functional — only `count`, the value the hydration merge was built to protect, is not. Also makes the target-reached vibration check (`next === target`) skippable.
findings.suggestedFix: setCount((c) => { const next = c + 1; Vibration.vibrate(next === target ? (Platform.OS === 'ios' ? [0,50,100,200] : 400) : 40); return next; }); — or track `next` via a ref; the increment must be functional like lifetime/todayCount.
findings.correction: The race window is narrower than the description may suggest: only taps landing in the few-millisecond gap between the AsyncStorage promise resolving and the hydration render committing are affected — taps before the promise resolves are still merged correctly by the functional hydration update. Per-session probability is therefore low, though the exposed scenario (rapid tapping immediately on open, slow device) is precisely the one the merge was built for, and the clobbered value is persisted. Medium severity is at the upper bound but defensible. Note for the fix: simply making increment functional removes the synchronous `next` needed for the target-vibration check, so the fix should derive the vibration from a ref or from the functional updater's input rather than a pre-computed `next`.

findings.title: EAS production build can fail at the new Sentry source-map upload step (no project configured, auth token only in gitignored .env.local)
findings.description: Commit 4604cde added the @sentry/react-native Expo plugin, which injects 'apply from: ... sentry.gradle' into android/app/build.gradle and writes android/sentry.properties at prebuild. The plugin config sets only url+organization — no 'project' key — so the properties file falls back to the SENTRY_PROJECT env var, and the auth token falls back to SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN. Verified in node_modules/@sentry/react-native/sentry.gradle: the upload task is wired 'bundleTask.finalizedBy cliTask', runs on every release bundle unless SENTRY_DISABLE_AUTO_UPLOAD=true, and invokes sentry-cli inside a Gradle exec{} with no ignoreExitValue — a non-zero sentry-cli exit (missing auth token / missing project) throws and FAILS the build. SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN exists only in .env.local and SENTRY_PROJECT exists nowhere in the repo; .gitignore excludes '.env', '.env.local', '.env.*.local', so EAS remote builds will not receive them unless they were manually added as EAS environment variables (the commit message says to do this, but nothing in the repo proves it was done). The very next planned action per project memory is 'eas build --platform android --profile production'.
findings.evidence: app.json: ["@sentry/react-native/expo", { "url": "https://sentry.io/", "organization": "abdul-rehman-5j" }] (no "project"); node_modules/@sentry/react-native/sentry.gradle: doLast { exec { ... commandLine(*osCompatibility, *args) } } with onlyIf { shouldSentryAutoUploadGeneral() } and bundleTask.finalizedBy cliTask; .gitignore: ".env\n.env.local\n.env.*.local"; grep shows SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN only in .env.local and SENTRY_PROJECT in no file.
findings.suggestedFix: Before the next production build: add "project": "<sentry-project-slug>" to the plugin block, and confirm SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN (and SENTRY_PROJECT if the plugin key is omitted) exist as EAS env vars for the production profile (eas env:list). As a safety net so a Sentry outage/missing token can never block a release, set SENTRY_DISABLE_AUTO_UPLOAD=true in eas.json production env until the secrets are verified, or document it as the rollback switch.
findings.correction: Two refinements. (1) The failure is even more certain than described: because 'project' is absent from the plugin config, the build fails on 'A project ID or slug is required' even if SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN were provided — adding the auth token to EAS alone would not fix it. Fix requires both adding "project": "<sentry-project-slug>" to the plugin config in app.json AND setting SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN as an EAS env var/secret (or setting SENTRY_DISABLE_AUTO_UPLOAD=true to defer source-map upload). (2) Severity nuance: this is a loud build-time failure of the release pipeline, not a silent runtime/user-facing defect; 'high' may fit better than 'critical', though it does block the very next planned action (eas build --platform android --profile production).

findings.title: Ads never recover in-session if first-launch UMP consent gathering fails — the in-code 'retry' claim is dead code, and granting consent via Sidebar privacy options never initializes the SDK
findings.description: initAds()'s comment says 'requestAdsConsent() may retry and call initAds() again later in the session', but grep shows the ONLY call site of both functions is the one-shot module-load IIFE in App.tsx (lines 108-111). consent.ts's failure path (gatherPromise = null 'allow a retry later in the session') is likewise never exercised — nothing calls requestAdsConsent a second time. Consequence: on a first launch where requestInfoUpdate times out at the 4s cap or fails (dead/slow network), there is no persisted UMP state, canRequestAds() returns false, initAds() returns before resolveAdsReady, and whenAdsInitialized() never resolves — every AdBanner in the app stays adsReady=false for the entire session even after connectivity returns. The new AdBanner 30s–5m retry ladder never engages because it sits behind the unresolved promise. Additionally, an EEA user who initially declines and later grants consent via the new Sidebar 'Ad privacy options' entry (showPrivacyOptions) still gets zero ads until a full app restart, since nothing re-runs initAds after the privacy form closes.
findings.suggestedFix: Wire an actual retry: after showPrivacyOptions() resolves in Sidebar, call requestAdsConsent().then(initAds); and add a single AppState 'active' (or network-regained) hook that re-runs requestAdsConsent()+initAds() while !initialized. Alternatively have AdBanner's mount effect call initAds() (it is idempotent) instead of only awaiting whenAdsInitialized().
findings.correction: Two corrections. (a) The Sidebar sub-claim is wrong in its main scenario: per Google's ConsentInformation docs and AdMob forum, canRequestAds() returns true when status is OBTAINED or NOT_REQUIRED — OBTAINED includes an explicit decline. Since App.tsx awaits the user-paced consent form before initAds(), an EEA user who declines still gets the SDK initialized in the same startup chain (limited ads), so later granting via Sidebar 'Ad privacy options' works without a restart. The only path where 'Sidebar never re-runs initAds' could matter is when initAds never ran (info-update failure) — but then isPrivacyOptionsRequired() cannot return REQUIRED either, so the Sidebar row (Sidebar.tsx:590, gated on adPrivacyRequired) does not render; that sub-claim is structurally true but has no realistic user-facing path. (b) Severity is inflated: high → medium. UMP state persists once any info update succeeds and the init chain re-runs on every cold start, so impact is confined to the first-ever-launch session on a dead/slow network (zero ad revenue for that one session) plus misleading comments. Fix is still cheap: re-run requestAdsConsent()+initAds() on network regain or app foreground while uninitialized, or delete the inaccurate retry comments.

findings.title: Hajj/Umrah checklist: toggle before AsyncStorage hydration wipes saved progress and the late hydration reverts the tap
findings.description: The new persistence (commit 7e8b532) restores completedIds in a mount effect but does not gate toggles on hydration (TakbirScreen in the same commit does: disabled={!hydrated}). If the user taps 'Mark as Complete' before getItem resolves (step 1 is default-expanded, and AsyncStorage can be slow on low-end Android right after cold start), toggleComplete builds 'next' from the still-empty Set and persists JSON.stringify([thatOneId]) — overwriting the previously saved array (multi-day Hajj progress lost on next launch). The in-flight getItem then resolves with the OLD raw value and setCompletedIds(new Set(parsed)) visually reverts the tap the user just made. UmrahGuideScreen.tsx lines 46-69 has the identical pattern.
findings.suggestedFix: Mirror TakbirScreen: add a hydrated state set in .finally() of the restore effect; either disable the done buttons until hydrated, or merge on hydration (union of stored ids and current in-memory ids) and use the functional setCompletedIds(prev => ...) form when toggling. Apply the same fix to UmrahGuideScreen.tsx.
findings.correction: Real but severity is slightly inflated; low-to-medium is more accurate. Two mitigating details: (a) these are stack screens reached via navigation, not the app's first screen, so AsyncStorage is already warm at mount and the getItem race window is typically tens of milliseconds, not a cold-start-length gap; (b) the failure partially self-heals — the late hydration visibly reverts the user's tap, and re-tapping after hydration rebuilds `next` from the restored full set and rewrites storage correctly. Permanent multi-day data loss requires the user to tap inside the window and then never toggle anything again post-hydration. The mechanism, both affected files, and the TakbirScreen contrast are all exactly as described; fix is the same hydrated flag (set in .finally) gating the toggle, as TakbirScreen already does.

findings.title: EidScreen countdown/EID-MUBARAK state has no day-rollover tick — stale across the exact Chand-Raat-to-Eid-morning transition the commit fixed elsewhere
findings.description: Commit 7e8b532 added a minute interval + AppState 'active' refresh to TakbirScreen and QurbaniScreen specifically because 'a mount-frozen date leaves the banner stale', but EidScreen — the screen whose headline fix was Eid-day correctness — still computes getNextEid() and daysUntil() once per render with no 'now' state, no interval, and no AppState listener. A user who opens the Eid Guide on the night before Eid and returns to the still-mounted screen after midnight (app backgrounded overnight, or screen left open) sees '1 DAYS' instead of 'EID MUBARAK / TODAY' until something else forces a re-render. Same staleness applies at the end of the grace window and on the DAY-N counter during tashreeq.
findings.evidence: EidScreen.tsx line 155: 'const nextEid = getNextEid();' inside the component body, and line 166: 'const days = nextEid ? daysUntil(nextEid.date) : null;' — the file imports no AppState and contains no setInterval, in contrast to QurbaniScreen.tsx lines 98-105 ('const id = setInterval(() => setNow(new Date()), 60 * 1000); const sub = AppState.addEventListener(...)') added by the same commit.
findings.suggestedFix: Add the same pattern used in TakbirScreen/QurbaniScreen: const [now, setNow] = useState(() => new Date()) refreshed by a 60s interval + AppState 'active' listener, and pass 'now' into getNextEid()/daysUntil().
findings.correction: Severity is at the high end of medium and the exposure is narrower than the description implies. EidScreen is a Stack.Screen (App.tsx:326-329), not a keep-alive tab, so it fully remounts and recomputes on every navigation to it — the bug only manifests when the Eid Guide is left on top of the navigation stack across midnight (e.g., app backgrounded on that screen on Chand Raat, resumed Eid morning). It also self-heals on any parent re-render: getNextEid() is unmemoized, so a single Fitr/Adha tab tap refreshes the banner. The stale-data window is real and lands on exactly the highest-traffic transition, but it is a narrower scenario than the always-mounted Qurbani/Takbir-style staleness the description analogizes to; low-to-medium severity is the fairer rating. The fix itself is trivial and consistent with 7e8b532's pattern: lift a `now` state with the same minute interval + AppState listener and pass it to getNextEid()/daysUntil().

findings.title: AdBanner retry budget is lifetime-per-screen: a successful load never resets 'attempt', so later failures on keep-alive tabs exhaust retries and hide the banner for the rest of the session
findings.description: The new retry ladder increments 'attempt' on every failure and stops retrying once attempt >= 4, but onAdLoaded only sets loaded=true and never resets attempt to 0. On keep-alive tab screens (mounted all session, banners auto-refresh), a banner that successfully loaded for hours but then hits its 5th cumulative no-fill/network failure disappears permanently — recreating the 'hidden for the whole session after a failure' behavior this change was meant to fix, just with a higher threshold.
findings.evidence: AdBanner.tsx: 'useEffect(() => {\n    if (!failed || attempt >= RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length) return;\n    const t = setTimeout(() => { setFailed(false); setLoaded(false); setAttempt((a) => a + 1); }, RETRY_DELAYS_MS[attempt]);' together with 'onAdLoaded={() => setLoaded(true)}' which never resets attempt.
findings.suggestedFix: In onAdLoaded, also setAttempt(0) so the backoff ladder restarts from 30s after any successful load.
findings.correction: Claim is accurate but rests on one unstated dependency: after a successful load, additional onAdFailedToLoad events only occur if banner auto-refresh is enabled for the AdMob ad unit (console setting; Google-optimized refresh is the default for new units, and refresh failures do fire onAdFailedToLoad). If refresh is disabled, the lifetime cap only bites during a cold-start failure streak, which is the intended capped behavior. Suggested fix is trivial: reset attempt (and failed) in onAdLoaded, i.e. onAdLoaded={() => { setLoaded(true); setAttempt(0); }}. Adjacent un-claimed issue noticed: a single refresh failure sets failed=true and immediately unmounts a still-displayed valid ad for the 30s+ backoff window.

findings.title: SimpleModeContext hydration can revert an early user toggle (no user-selected guard, unlike the fixed LanguageContext)
findings.description: Commit 4604cde added a userSelectedRef guard to LanguageContext so async hydration can never overwrite an explicit user choice, but SimpleModeContext (touched in the same commit) kept the unguarded pattern: if stored value is 'true' and the user toggles Large Text OFF before getItem resolves (slow storage at cold start), the late .then runs setSimpleMode(true) and silently re-enables it while storage now says 'false' — UI and persisted state disagree until the next toggle. Narrow window, but it is the exact race class the sweep fixed one file over.
findings.suggestedFix: Add the same userSelectedRef: set it in toggleSimpleMode before setSimpleMode, and bail out of the hydration .then when it is set (plus a mounted flag).
findings.correction: The trigger scenario is slightly mischaracterized: pre-hydration the switch displays OFF (default false), so the user cannot toggle it OFF as a first action. The divergent sequence requires stored 'true' plus two taps before getItem resolves (ON then OFF, leaving state false / storage 'false'), after which late hydration re-applies setSimpleMode(true) while storage says 'false'. This makes the window even narrower than described; the bug class and the LanguageContext asymmetry are otherwise accurate.

findings.title: Onboarding: Skip/finish before settings hydration resolves still clobbers a returning user's saved prayer settings; tapping the already-selected Sunni card resets a hydrated method to Karachi
findings.description: The new hydration effect (added so a re-onboard 'no longer clobbers' saved choices) seeds selections from storage, but finish() writes the CURRENT on-screen selections unconditionally. Two holes: (1) a re-onboarded user (this screen re-shows precisely when storage is slow or the flag read failed) who taps 'Skip — choose later' before the getPrayerSettings promise resolves persists the regional defaults (Karachi/Hanafi/sunni) over their saved method/madhab/school — the exact clobber the hydration was meant to prevent, and made more likely because the screen re-shows under slow-storage conditions; (2) selectSchool('sunni') unconditionally runs setSelectedMethod('Karachi'), so a hydrated non-Karachi method (e.g. ISNA) is discarded if the user taps the already-highlighted Sunni card on step 1.
findings.suggestedFix: Track a hydratedRef (set in the hydration .then/.catch) and have finish() await/short-wait it (or skip writing prayer settings when nothing was touched and hydration hasn't settled); in selectSchool, only reset the method when the school actually changed (school !== selectedSchool).
findings.correction: Accurate overall; two narrowing notes. (a) The skip-race window in hole 1 is tighter than implied: Skip does not exist on the first (language) step, so the user must tap Continue at least once before Skip is reachable — the race is two AsyncStorage reads vs. at least one human tap, plausible mainly under the same slow-storage conditions that caused the re-show. (b) In hole 2 only the calculation method is lost; the hydrated madhab survives because hydration sets madhabTouchedRef=true, which selectSchool respects — the claim's evidence quote could be misread as the madhab also being clobbered, but it correctly scopes the loss to the method.

findings.title: Qibla foreground loop re-fires the OS location permission request and unthrottled GPS on every app resume
findings.description: The sweep added an AppState listener that calls retry() whenever the app foregrounds while permissionDenied or usingFallback is true. retry() re-runs fetchLocation, whose first permission step is requestLocationPermissionOnce() — which is Location.requestForegroundPermissionsAsync(), a REQUEST, not a read-only check (useLocation.ts line 72). For a user who denied location, every single foreground of the app while Qibla is mounted re-triggers a permission request: on Android this re-opens the OS dialog unprompted (until the system auto-suppresses), exactly the nagging pattern the same sweep explicitly avoided for notifications in PrayerTrackerScreen ('Read-only check — never re-prompts'). For usingFallback users it also bypasses the 15-minute REFRESH_THROTTLE_MS (retry is the unthrottled variant; refreshIfStale exists precisely for resume paths) and burns a 10s GPS attempt on every foreground.
findings.suggestedFix: In the QiblaScreen foreground handler, call refreshIfStale() instead of retry(); and in useLocation.fetchLocation, do a read-only Location.getForegroundPermissionsAsync() first and only call requestLocationPermissionOnce() when status is undetermined (mirroring hasNotificationPermission's read-only pattern in notifications).
findings.correction: Two refinements, one each direction. (1) Mitigating: the dialog-nag half is Android-only and bounded — after the user denies once more, Android permanently suppresses the dialog and the call returns denied instantly and cheaply (and the denied path never reaches GPS, so permissionDenied users incur no GPS cost); on iOS there is no visible re-prompt at all. The persistent unbounded cost is the usingFallback-with-granted-permission case (10s GPS race every foreground). (2) Aggravating: the claim's "while Qibla is mounted" understates exposure — this app's tab screens are keep-alive (documented in the code's own comments), so after the first visit to Qibla the listener is active for the entire session and the Android dialog can pop while the user is on any tab. Correct fix: use a read-only Location.getForegroundPermissionsAsync() gate (matching PrayerTrackerScreen's pattern) and/or call refreshIfStale() instead of retry() in the QiblaScreen listener — or delete the listener entirely since useLocation already has a throttled resume listener.

findings.title: Permission-denied Qibla shows two stacked location cards with conflicting CTAs, one of which is a dead end
findings.description: On a fresh install with location denied, useLocation sets both usingFallback=true (no cache) and permissionDenied=true, and useCompass sets available=false (same permission). The screen then renders BOTH the new red 'Approximate — location unavailable / Tap to retry' card (line 229) AND the orange 'Compass needs location permission / Tap to open Settings' card (line 293). Two adjacent cards about the same problem give opposing instructions, and the red card's 'Tap to retry' is a dead end once Android permanently suppresses the prompt (request silently returns denied — nothing visible happens on tap). The retry CTA should only be shown for the GPS-timeout/fallback case; the permission-denied case should deep-link to Settings.
findings.suggestedFix: Branch the warning card on permissionDenied: when denied, set onPress to Linking.openSettings() and change the body copy to 'enable location in Settings'; suppress it entirely when the compass permission card is already showing the same message.
findings.correction: Two refinements: (1) The retry CTA is not a dead end in ALL denied states — on Android while canAskAgain is still true, tapping retry re-opens the OS permission dialog, which is useful; the dead end is specifically the permanently-suppressed state. (2) On iOS the dead end is worse than claimed: iOS never re-prompts after a single denial, so 'Tap to retry' is a silent no-op from the first denial onward, not just after permanent suppression. The suggested fix direction is right: gate the retry card on usingFallback && !permissionDenied, and let the permission-denied case be handled solely by the Settings deep-link card.

findings.title: Indo-Pak fallback notice claims 'showing Madinah script' but renders the Uthmani text in the IndoPak font
findings.description: When the IndoPak fetch fails (indopakTexts === []), the new notice tells the user Madinah script is being shown, and the reader falls back to ayah.arabic (Uthmani text). But arabicFont is still selected purely by `script === 'indopak'`, so the Uthmani fallback text is rendered with fontFamily 'NoorehudaQuran' (the IndoPak font) in both mushaf mode (line 854) and verse mode (line 912). Uthmani text uses codepoints the IndoPak font isn't designed for (U+0671 hamzat-wasl alef, Uthmani small marks — visible in the bismillah constants which differ per script), so exactly in the failure state this sweep set out to make honest, the reader shows Madinah text shaped by the wrong Quran font (missing/fallback glyphs or wrong mark positioning), while the notice asserts everything is fine.
findings.suggestedFix: When falling back (indopakText == null while script === 'indopak'), also fall back the font: use theme.typography.fontQuranUthmani for the fallback text (e.g. const effectiveFont = indopakText ? arabicFont : theme.typography.fontQuranUthmani), and apply the same to the bismillah.
findings.correction: Two minor scope notes: (1) the same wrong-font rendering also occurs transiently while indopakTexts === null (before the IndoPak fetch resolves), not only after failure — though no notice is shown then; (2) most Uthmani small marks (U+0670, U+0653, U+06DC, U+06E2, etc.) ARE present in NoorehudaRegular.ttf — the concrete missing codepoint is U+0671 alef wasla, which is frequent enough (nearly every ayah) that the practical impact stands. The bismillah header itself is unaffected since bismillahText is a script-specific constant.

findings.title: Stats retry banner is not RTL-aware: Urdu/Arabic error text renders left-aligned in a fixed LTR row
findings.description: The app deliberately does not flip I18nManager (I18nProvider: 'no-op forceRTL — we render bidirectionally instead'), so RTL strings need explicit textAlign/flexDirection — which the codebase does elsewhere (e.g. SacredJourneyScreen quoteBody: textAlign isUrdu ? 'right' : 'left', and I18nProvider exposes writingDirection/flexDirection for exactly this). The new error banner added by the sweep hardcodes an LTR row (errorBanner flexDirection: 'row' with the Retry button on the right) and errorText has no textAlign, so the Urdu ('اعداد و شمار لوڈ نہیں ہو سکے…') and Arabic ('تعذر تحميل الإحصائيات…') messages render left-aligned with their punctuation displaced — visibly wrong for the two RTL languages the banner was explicitly localized for.
findings.evidence:
{loadFailed && (
        <View style={styles.errorBanner}>
          <Text style={styles.errorText}>{errorLabels.loadError}</Text>
...
  errorBanner: {
    flexDirection: 'row',
...
  errorText: {
    flex: 1,
    fontSize: 13,
    color: theme.colors.textMuted,
    fontFamily: theme.typography.fontBody,
    lineHeight: 18,
  },
findings.suggestedFix: For language 'ur'/'ar', set errorText textAlign: 'right' (and writingDirection: 'rtl') and flip the banner row to 'row-reverse' — or consume useI18n()'s textAlign/flexDirection values.
findings.correction: Two refinements: (1) 'punctuation displaced' is overstated — the layout still resolves intra-paragraph bidi via first-strong, so character ordering and the trailing Urdu full stop render in correct RTL order; the actual defect is flush-left alignment of RTL text plus the un-mirrored row (Retry button pinned to the visual right, where RTL reading begins). (2) Severity is at the high end of medium: the banner only renders on a rare storage-read failure path, so low-to-medium is a fairer rating, though defensible given the strings were explicitly localized for the two RTL languages. Fix is one line each: add textAlign (or writingDirection) from I18nProvider to errorText, and optionally flexDirection from I18nProvider on errorBanner.

findings.title: New retry touchables in QuranScreen fallback notices and MushafPageReader lack accessibilityRole/label, unlike the same sweep's Stats retry
findings.description: The sweep set the a11y bar itself: StatsScreen's retry got accessibilityRole="button", accessibilityLabel and hitSlop. But the two new QuranScreen fallback-notice touchables (lines 789-799 and 802-812) and the new MushafPageReader retry button (lines 397-403) ship with no accessibilityRole or label, so TalkBack/VoiceOver announces them as plain text — the tap-to-retry affordance is undiscoverable to screen-reader users on precisely the error-recovery paths this sweep added.
findings.suggestedFix: Add accessibilityRole="button" (plus an accessibilityLabel matching the visible text) to both QuranScreen fallback notices and the MushafPageReader retry button, mirroring StatsScreen.tsx lines 135-140.
findings.correction: Minor overstatement in impact wording: TouchableOpacity is accessible={true} by default in React Native, so screen readers do focus these elements as single units and read their inner text — and the QuranScreen notices' visible text literally says "Tap to retry" (English and Urdu), so the retry affordance is announced via content, just not via a "button" role trait. The MushafPageReader "Retry" button is the weaker case (short label, no role). So the affordance is degraded/inconsistent rather than "undiscoverable". The fix (add accessibilityRole="button" and labels, matching StatsScreen) is still correct.

findings.title: New 'Due now' prayer badge fails WCAG AA contrast (~3.2:1) — the only one of the four statuses that does
findings.description: The sweep's new 'due' status renders 13px badge text in theme accent #C8780A on accentMuted 'rgba(200, 120, 10, 0.12)' over the white surface — an effective contrast of about 3.2:1, below the 4.5:1 WCAG AA requirement for text this size. The pre-existing badges all pass: prayed success #1A7A3C ≈ 5.9:1, missed error #B83025 ≈ 6.3:1, upcoming textMuted #7A5A40 ≈ 6.2:1. The new state — the one actively prompting the user to act — is the hardest to read, especially outdoors at prayer time.
findings.suggestedFix: Use a darker accent for the badge text (e.g. ~#9B5D06 gives >4.5:1 on the muted background) or reuse textSecondary #3C2510 for the label while keeping the accent icon/border.
findings.correction: Two corrections to the claim's numbers. (1) The 'due' contrast is actually ~3.0:1 against the real composited badge background, slightly worse than the claimed ~3.2:1 (3.42:1 only if measured against pure white). (2) The comparative figures for the other badges were computed against white rather than their muted badge backgrounds and are inflated: actual values are prayed 4.44:1, missed 4.91:1, upcoming 5.59:1. Consequently the headline 'the only one of the four statuses that does [fail]' is false — the prayed/success badge (#1A7A3C on successMuted over white) also marginally fails AA at 4.44:1. The 'due' badge remains by far the worst and the core finding stands; a fix should consider darkening the prayed badge text too (or its background alpha).

findings.title: Eid countdown card shows the English Eid name in Arabic UI while every surrounding string was just localized to Arabic
findings.description: The sweep added isArabic branches to the card's label ('العيد القادم'), disclaimer ('* تقريبي — حسب رؤية الهلال'), days label and Eid Mubarak state — but the event name itself still only branches on isUrdu, so an Arabic user sees 'Eid ul-Fitr 2027' in Latin script sandwiched between two Arabic lines. The Arabic names are trivially derivable (the tab labels on the same screen already have them: 'عيد الفطر' / 'عيد الأضحى').
findings.evidence:
<Text style={[styles.countdownName, { fontSize: fs(15) }]}>
              {isUrdu ? nextEid.nameUr : nextEid.name}
            </Text>
findings.suggestedFix: Add a nameAr field to UPCOMING_EID_DATES in eidGuide.ts (e.g. 'عيد الفطر ٢٠٢٧') and render {isUrdu ? nextEid.nameUr : isArabic ? nextEid.nameAr : nextEid.name}.
findings.correction: Minor: the claim frames this as a missed ternary, but the EidDate type in src/constants/eidGuide.ts (lines 660-684) has no nameAr field — only name and nameUr. Fixing it requires either adding nameAr to all 11 UPCOMING_EID_DATES entries or deriving the Arabic name from the type field ('عيد الفطر'/'عيد الأضحى', already present as tab labels at EidScreen lines 235/244) plus the year. Slightly more than a one-line change, but still trivial.

findings.title: Urdu copy-confirmation string is machine-sounding and inconsistent with its own error branch ('نقل' vs 'کاپی')
findings.description: The new onCopyDua success toast in both HajjGuideScreen (line 61) and UmrahGuideScreen (line 74) says '✓ کلپ بورڈ پر نقل ہو گیا' — 'نقل' is a literary/Arabic-register word that reads like machine translation in app UI, and the very next line of the same function uses the everyday term for the failure case ('کاپی نہیں ہو سکا'). Same action, two different verbs. Also, the confirmation is a blocking Alert with an empty title (Alert.alert('', …)) that the user must dismiss after every copy — heavyweight for what other apps surface as a toast.
findings.suggestedFix: Use '✓ کاپی ہو گیا' for the success message in both guide screens to match the error branch's register; ideally replace the empty-title Alert with ToastAndroid/a transient inline '✓ Copied' state like a toast.
findings.correction: Minor refinements: (1) 'نقل' is not incorrect Urdu — it is a valid, common word for copying — so 'machine-sounding' slightly overstates it; the solid, objective issue is register/consistency: the same screens use 'کاپی' in the button label, accessibility label, and error message, making the success toast the lone outlier. (2) This code was introduced by round-1 fix commit 7e8b532, so it is a polish gap in the audit fix itself, not pre-existing copy. (3) The 'blocking Alert vs toast' point is accurate but RN core has no cross-platform toast; a fix would use ToastAndroid/a snackbar component, matching the existing QurbaniScreen Alert pattern otherwise.

findings.title: Urdu 'Day N of M' hero string uses machine-ordered phrasing and Latin digits against the app's Urdu-numeral convention
findings.description: The new HAPPENING NOW hero (line 487) and its share string (line 227) render Urdu as `${ongoing.total} میں سے دن ${ongoing.dayN}` → '30 میں سے دن 5'. The word order is calque-English ('of 30, day 5'); natural Urdu is '۳۰ میں سے ۵واں دن' or 'دن ۵ از ۳۰'. It also embeds Latin digits in an RTL sentence, while Urdu strings added in this same sweep use Eastern Arabic-Indic numerals (QurbaniScreen: '۷ میں سے … حصے', HomeScreen goals: '۱۰ منٹ قرآن کی تلاوت') — so Ramadan's month-long hero, the most visible RTL surface, is the inconsistent one.
findings.evidence:
? isUrdu
                  ? `${ongoing.total} میں سے دن ${ongoing.dayN}`
                  : isArabic
                  ? `اليوم ${ongoing.dayN} من ${ongoing.total}`
                  : `Day ${ongoing.dayN} of ${ongoing.total}`
findings.suggestedFix: Reword to `${toUrduDigits(ongoing.total)} میں سے ${toUrduDigits(ongoing.dayN)}واں دن` (adding a small Western→Urdu digit mapper, or Intl.NumberFormat('ur-PK')) and apply the same string in handleShare at line 227.
findings.correction: Two refinements. (1) The 'X میں سے' word order is NOT a calque — it is natural Urdu and identical to the structure QurbaniScreen itself uses ('۷ میں سے ... حصے'). The genuine phrasing flaw is only 'دن ${N}' (English 'Day N' noun-before-number order); natural Urdu wants the ordinal '${N}واں دن' or 'دن نمبر ${N}'. (2) The Latin-digit 'convention' violation only holds against hardcoded literals: QurbaniScreen's own interpolated count (${Math.min(people, 7)}) also renders Latin digits, and no toUrduDigits-style helper exists anywhere in src — so all dynamic numbers app-wide are Latin. The hero is inconsistent with literal-numeral strings, but consistent with every other interpolated value; a proper fix would add a shared digit-localization helper rather than patching this one string.

findings.title: Ads SDK init is a one-shot dead end: consent-gated initAds() is never retried, so a slow first-launch consent check disables ads for the whole session
findings.description: The sweep (4604cde) added a UMP gate inside initAds(): if canRequestAds() is false it returns without initializing and without resolving adsReadyPromise. But initAds() is called exactly once in the entire app — in the module-load IIFE in App.tsx:108-111 — immediately after requestAdsConsent(), whose network step (requestInfoUpdate) is capped at 4s. On a first launch with a slow network (or any session where UMP consent info is still UNKNOWN when the 4s cap fires), canRequestAds() returns false, initAds() bails, and nothing ever calls it again: whenAdsInitialized() never resolves and every AdBanner on all 17 screens stays hidden for the entire session, even though the native requestInfoUpdate typically completes seconds later and persists a canRequestAds=true state. The in-code comment explicitly claims 'requestAdsConsent() may retry and call initAds() again later in the session' — no such retry exists (grep confirms App.tsx:109-110 are the only call sites; consent.ts's gatherPromise=null retry-enable is also never exercised). At base 1b90f20, initAds() initialized unconditionally, so this whole-session ads outage is sweep-introduced.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a real retry path: e.g. in App.tsx, after the initial chain, listen for AppState 'active' (or set a short interval/backoff) and call initAds() again while !initialized; initAds() is already idempotent. Alternatively have requestAdsConsent() call initAds() itself once consent gathering completes (which would make the existing comment true).
findings.correction: Accurate overall; one severity nuance. Because the UMP SDK persists consent state natively across sessions, the whole-session ads outage is largely confined to (a) first-launch sessions where requestInfoUpdate exceeds the 4s cap or fails, and (b) sessions where getConsentInfo() throws; affected users recover on their next app launch. So 'high' is the upper bound — medium-high is a fairer rating. Minor addition strengthening the finding: in the 4s-timeout case the gatherPromise retry latch in consent.ts can never fire even in principle, because withTimeout resolves null rather than rejecting, so gatherPromise is never cleared. Fix direction: have requestAdsConsent() (or an AppState/foreground listener, or AdBanner mount) re-check canRequestAds() and call initAds() again, or poll/re-gate inside initAds() instead of one-shot returning.

findings.title: Language switch never re-runs the prayer/sacred notification scheduler: scheduleKey omits language despite language being in the effect deps
findings.description: PrayerTrackerScreen's scheduling effect (lines 230-291) lists `language` in its dependency array and passes `language` to both schedulePrayerNotificationsAhead and scheduleSacredCountdownNotifications — but the run-once guard key is `${todayKey}|${calculationMethod}|${madhab}|${lat}|${lng}`, with no language component. When the user switches language, the effect re-fires, matches lastScheduledKey, and early-returns at line 237 — so neither schedule is rebuilt. LanguageContext.setLanguage only reschedules streak + jumu'ah (LanguageContext.tsx:64-65). Net effect: the 5-day prayer reminders stay in the old language until the next app background/resume (App.tsx's rebuildPrayerScheduleFromStorage reads app_language), and the up-to-26 sacred-countdown notifications — date-triggered up to weeks out — keep firing in the OLD language until the next day rollover with the Prayers tab focused, or a SacredJourney mute/region interaction. An Urdu user switching to English (or vice versa) keeps getting Eid/Arafah/Ramadan pushes in the wrong language.
findings.suggestedFix: Append `|${language}` to scheduleKey so the language dep actually triggers a rebuild. Optionally also call rebuildPrayerScheduleFromStorage() and a sacred reschedule from LanguageContext.setLanguage so the fix doesn't depend on the Prayers tab being mounted.
findings.correction: Claim is accurate as written. Minor framing note only: the wrong-language exposure for sacred-countdown pushes is bounded — the key includes todayKey, so the next day the effect reschedules in the new language (if the Prayers tab updates `today`). Only sacred events firing within that window (e.g., language switched the evening before an event) actually deliver in the old language; "weeks" describes how far ahead notifications are scheduled, not the realistic bug window for an active user. The natural fix is to append language to scheduleKey at line 236.

findings.title: CACHE_VERSION bump v2→v3 permanently orphans all quran_surah_v2_* caches: the one-shot purge already ran on existing installs and its filter only matches the new prefix
findings.description: Commit d8bdc72 bumped CACHE_VERSION from 2 to 3 (verified: base 1b90f20 has `const CACHE_VERSION = 2`). readCache/writeCache now use quran_surah_v3_* keys only. The cleanup path, purgeQuranCacheOnce(), is gated by the flag 'quran_cache_purged_v1' which shipped pre-sweep (commit 8790872) — on every existing install it is already '1', so the purge never runs again; and even if it did, its filter uses the current CACHE_PREFIX (`quran_surah_v3_`) so v2 keys would still not match. Result: every surah a user cached since the original purge survives as permanently unreadable dead bytes in AsyncStorage, counting against Android's 6MB SQLite cap — the exact cap whose exhaustion (SQLITE_FULL) previously broke ALL app writes (streak, goals, prayer marks). It also makes HomeScreen's SQLITE_FULL recovery alert ('it'll clean itself up on next launch', HomeScreen.tsx:263) untrue, since the one-shot flag means no future launch ever purges anything. Supporting evidence that a version migration was intended but never wired: CACHE_VERSION_KEY ('quran_cache_version', quran.ts:9) is declared and never read or written anywhere in the repo.
findings.suggestedFix: Use the unused CACHE_VERSION_KEY: on startup compare the stored version with CACHE_VERSION and, when different, multiRemove all keys matching the generic 'quran_surah_v' prefix except the current CACHE_PREFIX (same for 'quran_tajweed_v'), then store the new version. Alternatively change the purge filter to generic 'quran_surah_v' / version-agnostic prefixes and key the purge flag to CACHE_VERSION (e.g. 'quran_cache_purged_v3') so each version bump re-runs it once.
findings.correction: The claim slightly UNDERSTATES the bug: orphaning does not depend on the flag already being '1'. Even on installs where the purge has never run (e.g., a user upgrading directly from a prefetch-era v2 build to a v3 build), the first-run purge still misses every quran_surah_v2_* key because the filter is built from the current CACHE_PREFIX — so the users the purge was written for (those with ~5-6MB of prefetch bloat) get zero reclamation if they skip the intermediate purge builds, and the flag then locks that state in permanently. Note the filter already does this correctly for page caches (`k.startsWith('quran_page_v')` is version-agnostic); the natural fix is the same version-agnostic prefix `'quran_surah_v'` for surah keys (plus re-running on version change via the currently-dead CACHE_VERSION_KEY).

findings.title: useDayKey instantiated three times on the keep-alive Home tab: 3 duplicate AppState listeners + 3 perpetual 60-second intervals
findings.description: useDayKey() is called independently by HomeScreen (HomeScreen.tsx:112), TodayCard (TodayCard.tsx:92), and HadithOfDay (HadithOfDay.tsx:17) — all mounted simultaneously and permanently on the keep-alive Home tab. Each instance registers its own AppState 'change' listener and its own setInterval(refresh, 60_000) that runs for the entire app session. Correctness is unaffected (the setState bails out when the key is unchanged, and all three flip atomically at midnight), but the app carries 3x redundant timers and listeners forever. No missed-refresh or double-refresh bug was found in the rollover logic itself.
findings.suggestedFix: Compute dayKey once in HomeScreen (it already calls useDayKey) and pass it as a prop to TodayCard and HadithOfDay, or lift useDayKey into a small context/singleton subscription so only one timer + listener exists.
findings.correction: Minor overstatement only: the intervals are not truly 'perpetual' — React Native suspends JS timers while the app is backgrounded, so the 3 intervals tick only during foreground time, and each tick is a trivial date-key string comparison. Practical cost is negligible (code-hygiene level); the natural cleanup is a single shared subscription (module-level emitter or a DayKeyContext provided once by HomeScreen) rather than per-hook listeners.

findings.title: Cache version bump v2→v3 orphans every pre-sweep surah cache forever — the one-shot purge already ran and can never reclaim them
findings.description: The fix sweep bumped CACHE_VERSION from 2 to 3 (commit d8bdc72) to invalidate Bismillah-poisoned/translation-less caches, but nothing deletes the old `quran_surah_v2_*` AsyncStorage entries. purgeQuranCacheOnce() is guarded by the flag `quran_cache_purged_v1`, which shipped in an earlier release and is already '1' on every upgraded install — so it never runs again. Even if it did, its filter matches `CACHE_PREFIX` (now `quran_surah_v3_`), so v2 keys would still be missed; `quran_tajweed_v1_*` entries (from the pre-v2 tajweed bump) were never in the filter at all. Every surah a user cached since the original purge is now permanent dead weight, with fresh v3 copies accumulating alongside. The file's own comments document exactly why this matters: Android AsyncStorage's ~6MB SQLite cap, where filling it makes EVERY other write in the app (streak, goals, prayer marks) fail with SQLITE_FULL. The defined-but-unused CACHE_VERSION_KEY ('quran_cache_version') shows a version-tracked cleanup was intended but never wired up.
findings.suggestedFix: Store the current cache version under CACHE_VERSION_KEY; on startup, when the stored version differs, remove all keys matching /^quran_surah_v/ and /^quran_tajweed_v/ that don't carry the current prefixes, then write the new version. This also future-proofs the next bump.
findings.correction: Two details are wrong but don't change the verdict: (1) 'quran_tajweed_v1_*' keys never existed — the pre-v2 tajweed prefix was unversioned 'quran_tajweed_' and its bump to 'quran_tajweed_v2_' landed in pre-launch commit 2258706, so no production device holds orphaned tajweed keys from that bump (current tajweed keys are simply excluded from the purge by design). (2) The v3 bump has not shipped yet — last release is 1.0.7/vc 9 and d8bdc72 is newer — so the orphaning is pending rather than already done; it can still be prevented before the next release (e.g., bump the purge flag with a filter that also matches quran_surah_v2_, or wire up the unused CACHE_VERSION_KEY).

findings.title: Mushaf reader renders the sajdah mark ۩ twice on all 14 listed sajdah verses — the API text already embeds it
findings.description: MushafPage appends its own styled ' ۩ ' after any verse where isSajdahAyah() is true, but the Quran.com text the reader displays already contains the ۩ symbol inline as part of the rasm — verified live for both fields the reader uses: text_uthmani for 32:15 and 16:50 ends with '۩', and text_indopak for 32:15 ends with 'يَسۡتَكۡبِرُوۡنَ۩'. So every sajdah verse in the 604-page Mushaf shows the prostration mark twice in a row (…۩ ۩ ﴿15﴾) — a visible defect in the core Quran text that any hafiz/regular reader will notice, on exactly the 14 most religiously significant verses for prostration. Meanwhile 22:77 (whose API text also carries ۩) gets only the un-styled inline mark because it's missing from SAJDAH_VERSES, making the styling inconsistent between sajdah verses.
findings.suggestedFix: Either strip the trailing ۩ from the source text and keep only the app-styled mark, or drop the appended mark and instead color/annotate verses where the text already contains ۩ (which also automatically covers 22:77).
findings.correction: One refinement: in IndoPak script mode the duplication hits 13 of 14 verses, not all 14 — Quran.com's text_indopak for 38:24 (Sad) does NOT embed ۩ (verified live), so that verse shows only the single appended mark in IndoPak mode; all 14 duplicate in Uthmani (the default for the 604-page reader). Also note the 22:77 omission is a deliberate fiqh choice documented in sajdahList.ts (14-sajdah classical count incl. 38:24 with a madhhab note), but since the rendered rasm marks 15 positions including 22:77, the visual inconsistency the claim describes is real regardless. Suggested fix direction: stop appending the extra mark (the rasm already carries it) and instead style/detect the embedded U+06E9, or strip the embedded glyph before appending — and bump the page cache version since fetched pages are persisted in AsyncStorage.

findings.title: SAJDAH_VERSES omits Al-Hajj 22:77 despite claiming to match the Madinah Mushaf legend (which marks 15 sajdahs)
findings.description: The file's header says the list follows 'the standardized Madinah Mushaf legend (King Fahd Glorious Quran Printing Complex)' and that 'the exact ayah marker (the ۩ symbol) … matches these references'. That is factually wrong: the Madinah Mushaf marks 15 prostration places including the second sajdah of Surat al-Hajj at 22:77 (verified live — Quran.com's KFGQPC-derived text_uthmani for 22:77 ends with ۩). 22:77 is the disputed one the Shafi'i/Hanbali schools count as a sajdah of recitation (they exclude 38:24 instead); the list models the 38:24 disagreement with a fiqh note but silently drops 22:77 entirely. Users following along in a physical Madinah mushaf will see a ۩ at 22:77 that the app's sajdah indicator (isSajdahAyah in MushafPageReader) does not acknowledge.
findings.evidence:
export const SAJDAH_VERSES: SajdahVerse[] = [
  { surah: 7,  ayah: 206 },
  { surah: 13, ayah: 15 },
  { surah: 16, ayah: 50 },
  { surah: 17, ayah: 109 },
  { surah: 19, ayah: 58 },
  { surah: 22, ayah: 18 },   // ← no 22:77 entry follows
  { surah: 25, ayah: 60 },
… — live API 22:77 text_uthmani: '…لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ ۩' (HAS_SAJDAH_MARK: True)
findings.suggestedFix: Add { surah: 22, ayah: 77, note: 'Counted as a sajdah of recitation by Shafi\'i & Hanbali; Hanafi & Maliki do not.' } and correct the header comment (Madinah mushaf marks 15 places, schools differ on 22:77 vs 38:24).
findings.correction: Claim is accurate as stated. One additional nuance the audit missed: the header's line 'The classical schools agree on 14 of these' is also wrong — the schools agree on roughly 13/14 with the disagreement running in opposite directions (Hanafi count includes 38:24 and excludes 22:77; Shafi'i/Hanbali include 22:77 and exclude 38:24), while the Madinah Mushaf marks all 15 places. A proper fix should add { surah: 22, ayah: 77 } with a fiqh note (mirroring the existing 38:24 note) and correct the header comment, rather than just appending the entry.

findings.title: Tapping the 'Sunni' card in onboarding silently resets a previously saved/selected calculation method to Karachi — defeating the sweep's own re-onboard hydration fix
findings.description: Commit 4604cde added hydration that seeds the onboarding selections from stored prayer settings specifically so a re-shown onboarding (slow/failed flag read) can't 'silently reset the user's calculation method' (its own comment). But selectSchool('sunni') unconditionally calls setSelectedMethod('Karachi'). Two concrete failure paths: (1) re-onboarded user whose stored method was hydrated (e.g. NorthAmerica, Egyptian, UmmAlQura) taps the already-selected Sunni card to proceed → method silently becomes Karachi and finish() persists it, shifting their prayer/notification times for good; (2) normal flow: user reaches step 2, picks Egyptian, taps Back, re-taps Sunni → choice is wiped back to Karachi. The madhab is protected by madhabTouchedRef in this exact code path, but the method has no equivalent guard.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a methodTouchedRef mirroring madhabTouchedRef (set in selectMethod and by hydration when a stored method is found); in selectSchool's sunni branch only reset the method when it is currently 'Jafari' (i.e. when switching away from Shia actually requires it).
findings.correction: Claim is accurate as stated. Minor additions: (1) the worst variant is tapping Sunni then "Skip — choose later" on the school step — the method list is never displayed, so the reset is fully invisible; in the normal flow the user does land on step 2 where Karachi shows checked, so an attentive user could notice before persisting. (2) In path 1 the user is not forced to tap the Sunni card (Continue proceeds without it), so the trigger is a redundant-but-common confirm tap, consistent with medium rather than high severity. (3) A related unclaimed quirk: selectSchool('shia') also unconditionally resets madhab to 'Shafi' even when madhabTouchedRef is set, though madhab is hidden/irrelevant for the Jafari method UI.

findings.title: Ads SDK init is a single cold-start shot: if the 4s-capped UMP info update hasn't produced canRequestAds yet, ads stay off for the entire session and whenAdsInitialized never resolves
findings.description: initAds() now (4604cde) bails out when canRequestAds() is false, and its comment claims 'requestAdsConsent() may retry and call initAds() again later in the session' — but no such call exists. The only invocations of requestAdsConsent() and initAds() in the codebase are the one module-load IIFE in App.tsx (lines 108-111). consent.ts's retry affordance ('a failed attempt clears the in-flight promise so the next call retries') is dead code for the same reason — there is no next call. Consequence: on any launch where UMP has no persisted consent state yet (first install) and requestInfoUpdate exceeds its 4s cap — a routine event on the app's flaky-network Pakistan/India audience — canRequestAds() is false at the moment initAds runs, the gate returns, adsReadyPromise never resolves, and every AdBanner stays hidden (adsReady never true) for the whole session even after connectivity returns seconds later. Zero ad revenue for exactly the sessions/markets the round-1 ads fixes were meant to recover, with nothing captured in Sentry (the early return isn't reported).
findings.suggestedFix: Re-attempt on AppState 'active' (throttled) and/or have AdBanner's mount call a retryAdsInit() that re-runs requestAdsConsent()+initAds() when not yet initialized; also captureError/breadcrumb when the canRequestAds gate blocks init so the failure mode is visible.
findings.correction: Minor: the consent.ts retry affordance is doubly dead, not just dead for lack of a second caller. withTimeout (consent.ts:28-33) resolves null on timeout instead of rejecting, so the 4s-cap path never hits the catch that clears gatherPromise (consent.ts:69) — even a hypothetical later call to requestAdsConsent() would await the stale fulfilled promise and never re-run requestInfoUpdate. A fix needs both a retry trigger (e.g., on app foreground/connectivity regain) and timeout-aware clearing of gatherPromise. Impact is limited to first-install sessions (UMP persists state across launches), which supports the medium severity.

findings.title: Tasbih virtue texts attach ﷺ to Prophets Ibrahim and Yunus in all three languages — wrong honorific a practicing user will immediately notice
findings.description: By universal convention ﷺ (ṣallallāhu ʿalayhi wa-sallam) is reserved for Prophet Muhammad; other prophets take عليه السلام (AS). The 'hasbunallah' preset writes 'Ibrahim ﷺ' (and 'حضرت ابراہیم ﷺ' / 'إبراهيم ﷺ'), and the 'dua_yunus' preset writes 'Yunus ﷺ' (and 'حضرت یونس ﷺ' / 'يونس ﷺ') — six strings across en/ur/ar. The app's own newer content gets this right: the Urdu/Arabic mood-coach pools added in commit 4604cde use 'ابراہیم علیہ السلام' and 'يونس عليه السلام' for the same stories. This is exactly the kind of content error that costs credibility (and review-bombs) in an Islamic app.
findings.evidence:
virtue: 'Said by Ibrahim ﷺ in the fire and by the Prophet ﷺ at Uhud — a refuge in hardship (Quran 3:173, Bukhari 4563)',
…
virtue: 'The dua of Yunus ﷺ from the belly of the whale — whoever calls with it, Allah answers (Quran 21:87, Tirmidhi 3505)',
— contrast src/services/ai.ts (same sweep): 'ابتُلي إبراهيم عليه السلام بالنار، ويونس عليه السلام بالظلمات'
findings.suggestedFix: Replace ﷺ with (AS) / علیہ السلام / عليه السلام for Ibrahim and Yunus in lines 131-133 and 143-145, keeping ﷺ only for the Prophet Muhammad references.

findings.title: Waqf legend rows fall back to English for Arabic-language users even though the modal's own header/close are localized to Arabic
findings.description: WaqfLegendModal receives `language` and branches on isArabic for the title ('علامات الوقف'), subtitle, and Close button — but every actual legend row and both section headers branch only `isUrdu ? … : <English>`. An Arabic user opening the legend sees an Arabic title followed by 'Must pause. Continuing here can change the meaning…', 'Pause / Stop Marks', 'Other Markers', and the English footnote. The data model has no nameAr/meaningAr fields at all, so this can't be fixed at the call site. Ironic for this specific audience: waqf-mark names ARE Arabic terms, so the Arabic branch is the easiest of the three to author.
findings.evidence:
<Text style={[styles.rowName, { color: tc.fg }]}>
  {isUrdu ? e.nameUr : e.nameEn}
</Text>
<Text style={styles.rowMeaning}>
  {isUrdu ? e.meaningUr : e.meaningEn}
</Text>
…
<Text style={styles.sectionTitle}>
  {isUrdu ? 'وقف کی علامات' : 'Pause / Stop Marks'}
</Text>
— while the same component renders {isArabic ? 'علامات الوقف' : …} for the title and {isArabic ? 'إغلاق' : 'Close'}.
findings.suggestedFix: Add nameAr/meaningAr to LegendEntry (the names are Arabic already; meanings are short) and branch isArabic in renderRow, the two section titles, and the footnote.
findings.correction: Claim is accurate as stated. One minor refinement: it is not only the legend rows and section headers — the footnote (lines 220-224) also lacks an Arabic branch, so everything below the header except the Close button falls back to English for 'ar' users. Fix would require adding nameAr/meaningAr to LegendEntry plus Arabic strings for the two section titles and the footnote.

findings.title: Dua Library 'Copy Arabic' gives zero feedback — same defect the sweep fixed in Hajj/Janaza/Umrah guides, missed here
findings.description: Commit 7e8b532 added copy confirmation (Alert '✓ Copied to clipboard') and a failure branch to the guide screens (e.g. HajjGuideScreen.onCopyDua), but DuaScreen's detail-sheet Copy button still fires Clipboard.setStringAsync bare: no success toast, and a rejected promise is completely unhandled (unhandled rejection + user believes the dua was copied when it wasn't). This is the highest-traffic copy button in the app (every dua detail sheet) and is now inconsistent with the rest of the app's copy UX.
findings.evidence:
<TouchableOpacity
  style={styles.shareBtn}
  onPress={() => Clipboard.setStringAsync(selected.arabic)}
  accessibilityLabel={isUrdu ? 'عربی متن کاپی کریں' : 'Copy Arabic text'}
>
  <Text style={styles.shareBtnText}>📋 Copy Arabic</Text>
— vs HajjGuideScreen.tsx (same sweep): const onCopyDua = async (arabic) => { try { await Clipboard.setStringAsync(arabic); Alert.alert('', '✓ Copied to clipboard'); } catch { Alert.alert('Error', 'Could not copy'); } }
findings.suggestedFix: Reuse the guides' onCopyDua pattern (await + success alert/toast + catch with error alert), localized for ur/ar like the surrounding labels.
findings.correction: Two overstatements: (1) the 'unhandled rejection' risk is theoretical — expo-clipboard setStringAsync essentially never rejects for plain strings, and an unhandled rejection in React Native is a console warning, not a crash; the real defect is the missing copy confirmation and UX inconsistency. (2) 'highest-traffic copy button in the app' is unverified speculation. Also note this is a known round-1 leftover: the audit JSON's own correction (docs/audit-2026-06-10.json:836) already identified DuaScreen.tsx:223, but the fix commit 7e8b532 only covered the guide screens.

findings.title: AyahSheet dismisses on iOS 'inactive' (Control Center / notification shade), not only on real backgrounding
findings.description: The new AppState listener fires onClose() for any state !== 'active'. On iOS, AppState reports 'inactive' for transient interruptions — pulling down Control Center or the notification shade, the app switcher peek, Siri, an incoming-call banner. Each of those stops the ayah audio and closes the sheet entirely, so the user returns a second later to find their selected verse gone. The stated goal of the fix was only to stop lock-screen audio and avoid a stale sheet after true backgrounding.
findings.suggestedFix: Trigger only on state === 'background' (Android backgrounding reports 'background' directly; iOS reports 'background' shortly after a real backgrounding, while Control Center/shade only ever reach 'inactive'). Optionally pause instead of closing the sheet.
findings.correction: Minor framing error only: the claim calls it 'the new AppState listener' implying it was introduced by the round-1 audit fix. git log -S shows it was added earlier, in commit 037e27d ('AyahSheet parity with the surah reader'), before the audit; the round-1 fix d8bdc72 merely renamed a state setter inside it. The defect is pre-existing, not a regression from the audit fixes. The behavioral analysis is otherwise accurate. Suggested fix: only dismiss on state === 'background' (Android reports 'background' directly; iOS passes through 'inactive' first but reaches 'background' on true backgrounding), which preserves the lock-screen-audio goal while surviving Control Center/notification-shade peeks.

findings.title: Transliteration of #82 drops the final doubled consonant: 'Al-'Afu' should be 'Al-'Afuww'
findings.description: Entry 82 renders الْعَفُوّ as "Al-'Afu". The Arabic (correctly written in the file with a shadda on the waw) is pronounced 'Afuww (al-'Afuww), as in Quran 4:149 'afuwwan (عَفُوًّا). The file's own transliteration convention doubles final geminate consonants everywhere else — Al-'Aliyy (#36), Al-Qawiyy (#53), Al-Waliyy (#55), Al-Ghaniyy (#88), Al-Haqq (#51), Al-Hayy (#62), Al-Barr (#79) — so #82 is internally inconsistent and slightly misleads pronunciation ('Afu vs 'Afuww). Arabic text and meaning ('The Pardoner') are correct; only the romanization is affected.
findings.evidence: App: { number: 82, arabic: 'الْعَفُوّ', transliteration: "Al-'Afu", ... }. Authoritative rendering: sunnah.com's Tirmidhi 3507 list gives "Al-Afuww (العفو)"; the Quranic occurrence (4:149) is عَفُوًّا ('afuwwan) with shadda, confirming the geminate ww.
findings.suggestedFix: Change transliteration at line 91 from "Al-'Afu" to "Al-'Afuww" to match the Arabic shadda and the file's own convention (Al-'Aliyy, Al-Qawiyy, etc.).
findings.correction: In /Users/malikabdul/risepath/src/constants/asmaulHusna.ts line 91, change transliteration from "Al-'Afu" to "Al-'Afuww" to match the shadda in the Arabic and the file's own convention for geminate-final names.

findings.title: Dhul Hijjah 1448 block (Eid al-Adha 2027, Arafah, 1 Dhul Hijjah) is one day late vs Umm al-Qura and vs the file's own 1449 convention
findings.description: The file header (line 72) says base dates are 'anchored to Saudi/Umm al-Qura'. The 1448 Dhul Hijjah block uses 1 DhH = May 8 2027, Arafah = May 16 2027, Eid al-Adha = May 17 2027 — all +1 day vs Umm al-Qura (1 DhH 1448 = May 7, 9 DhH = May 15, 10 DhH = May 16, 2027). The parallel 1449 block (lines 228-239: Apr 26 / May 4 / May 5, 2028) matches UAQ exactly, so the two years follow different conventions within the same file. A user fasting the Day of Arafah per the app would fast May 16 when Saudi Arafah is May 15 — a wrong-day fast for anyone on the saudi/global region. The same May 17 date is repeated in eidGuide.ts line 676 ('Eid ul-Adha 2027'). The likely cause is chaining the +1 from the 30-day-Ramadan assumption (Eid al-Fitr Mar 10) through to Dhul Hijjah in 1448 but not in 1449 — and Dhul Hijjah dates should never inherit a Ramadan-length assumption since UAQ fixes them independently.
findings.evidence: App: baseDate d(2027, 5, 8) endDate d(2027, 5, 17) for dhj-1448; d(2027, 5, 16) for arafah-1448; d(2027, 5, 17) for adha-1448. Umm al-Qura via Aladhan (calendarMethod=UAQ): 01-12-1448 -> 07-05-2027, 10-12-1448 -> 16-05-2027 (so Arafah = 15-05-2027). Contrast: the file's own 1449 block matches UAQ exactly (01-12-1449 -> 26-04-2028, 10-12-1449 -> 05-05-2028, app uses Apr 26 / May 5). External sources also list Eid al-Adha 2027 in Saudi Arabia as Sunday May 16, 2027 (10 Dhul Hijjah 1448, Umm al-Qura).
findings.suggestedFix: Change dhj-1448 to baseDate d(2027, 5, 7) / endDate d(2027, 5, 16), arafah-1448 to d(2027, 5, 15), adha-1448 to d(2027, 5, 16); update eidGuide.ts UPCOMING_EID_DATES 'Eid ul-Adha 2027' to new Date(2027, 4, 16). If the team instead wants to keep the chained 30-day-Ramadan convention, apply it to 1449 Dhul Hijjah too and document it — but anchoring Dhul Hijjah to UAQ (as 1449 already does) is the correct fix.
findings.correction: In src/constants/islamicCalendar.ts shift the 1448 Dhul Hijjah block back one day to match Umm al-Qura: dhj-1448 baseDate d(2027,5,7) endDate d(2027,5,16); arafah-1448 baseDate d(2027,5,15); adha-1448 baseDate d(2027,5,16); update the comment to 'Eid al-Adha 1448 = May 16, 2027. 1 Dhul Hijjah = May 7.' Also update src/constants/eidGuide.ts:676 to new Date(2027,4,16). Separately consider fixing the related Eid al-Fitr 1448 entry (UAQ 1 Shawwal 1448 = Mar 9 2027, app has Mar 10, with Ramadan endDate Mar 9 -> should be Mar 8 per UAQ 29-day Ramadan) and the qadr-*-1448 night dates if they were derived from the 30-day assumption (per UAQ they are correct as-is since they fall within Ramadan; only the end/Eid dates drift).

findings.title: Shab-e-Barat 1448 is dated Jan 24, 2027 — one day late vs Umm al-Qura, and inconsistent with the file's own 1449 entry
findings.description: shabebarat-1448 has baseDate Jan 24, 2027, but Umm al-Qura puts 15 Sha'ban 1448 on Jan 23, 2027 (so the night of Bara'ah is the night of Jan 22-23 in Saudi). The file's own shabebarat-1449 entry (line 195, Jan 12, 2028) matches UAQ exactly (15 Sha'ban 1449 = 12-01-2028), proving the file's convention is the UAQ daytime date of 15 Sha'ban — making the 1448 entry internally inconsistent and wrong under the file's declared Saudi/UAQ anchor. Worse, the southasia +1 offset turns it into Jan 25 for Pakistani users, which is 16-17 Sha'ban under any regional convention — users would observe the worship night a day (or two) late.
findings.evidence: App: { id: 'shabebarat-1448', baseDate: d(2027, 1, 24) } vs UAQ: 15-08-1448 -> 23-01-2027. The sibling entry shabebarat-1449 d(2028, 1, 12) matches UAQ 15-08-1449 -> 12-01-2028 exactly.
findings.suggestedFix: Change shabebarat-1448 baseDate to d(2027, 1, 23) to match the UAQ date of 15 Sha'ban, consistent with the 1449 entry.
findings.correction: In /Users/malikabdul/risepath/src/constants/islamicCalendar.ts line 121, change shabebarat-1448 baseDate from d(2027, 1, 24) to d(2027, 1, 23) to match UAQ 15 Sha'ban 1448 (23-01-2027), consistent with the file's convention proven by the shabebarat-1449, ramadan-1448, and isra-1448 entries.

findings.title: UPCOMING_EID_DATES comment falsely claims Umm al-Qura agreement, and the list silently mixes two dating conventions
findings.description: The comment above UPCOMING_EID_DATES (lines 667-672) claims the entries were 'verified against the official Umm al-Qura tables (Aladhan hToG calendarMethod=UAQ...) and agree on all of them'. This is false for three entries: UAQ gives Eid al-Fitr 2027 = Mar 9 (app: Mar 10), Eid al-Adha 2027 = May 16 (app: May 17), Eid al-Fitr 2028 = Feb 26 (app: Feb 27). The 2027/2028 Fitr entries follow a 30-day-Ramadan convention (defensible on its own, and consistent with islamicCalendar.ts), but the 2029 and 2031 Fitr entries follow UAQ's 29-day Ramadans exactly (Feb 14 2029, Jan 24 2031), so the single user-facing list mixes a 30-day-Ramadan assumption for near years with a pure-UAQ assumption for far years. The Adha 2027 = May 17 entry is wrong under the UAQ standard the comment itself claims (see the separate Dhul Hijjah 1448 finding).
findings.evidence: Comment: 'verified against the official Umm al-Qura tables ... agree on all of them'. UAQ: 01-10-1448 -> 09-03-2027 (app Mar 10); 10-12-1448 -> 16-05-2027 (app May 17); 01-10-1449 -> 26-02-2028 (app Feb 27). Meanwhile UAQ Ramadan 1450 is 29 days (01-09-1450 -> 16-01-2029, 01-10-1450 -> 14-02-2029) and app uses Feb 14 2029 (pure UAQ, no 30-day padding); same for 2031 (UAQ Ramadan 1452 is 29 days, app Jan 24 2031 = UAQ exact).
